findings.title: skills.service.ts create() and update() missing P2002 catch — concurrent duplicate name causes unhandled 500
findings.description:
The `create()` method performs a pre-check `findFirst` then calls `prisma.skill.create()` without wrapping the create in a try/catch for Prisma P2002 (unique constraint violation). `Skill.name` has a `@unique` constraint in schema.prisma. Under concurrent requests (two users submitting the same skill name simultaneously), both pass the findFirst check, then the second `prisma.skill.create()` throws a `PrismaClientKnownRequestError` with code P2002 that is unhandled. This propagates as a 500 Internal Server Error. The same issue exists in `update()` at line 165 which also has no try/catch around `prisma.skill.update()`.

By contrast, `departments.service.ts` and `services.service.ts` explicitly define an `isUniqueViolation()` helper and wrap their create/update calls in try/catch blocks to map P2002 → 409 ConflictException. `skills.service.ts` lacks this defense.
findings.root_cause: `skills.service.ts` never adopted the P2002 catch pattern that `departments.service.ts` and `services.service.ts` introduced (COR-034), leaving the unique-constraint race unhandled.
findings.impact: A concurrent double-submit of a skill with the same name (or a client retry) returns HTTP 500 instead of 409, exposing an internal Prisma error in the response body and breaking any client that expects a structured conflict error.
acceptance_criteria: Two concurrent POST /skills with the same name both return HTTP 409 (not 500)
PATCH /skills/:id renaming to an existing name returns HTTP 409 under concurrency
findings.notes: departments.service.ts lines 12-19 and 69-74 show the correct pattern. importSkills() at line 725 already has a try/catch per item. Only the standalone create()/update() methods are missing it.

findings.title: services.service.ts remove() performs 3 sequential DB writes without a transaction — inconsistent state on crash
findings.description: The `remove()` method executes three separate database writes sequentially: (1) deleteMany on userService pivot rows, (2) update service.managerId to null, (3) delete the service. None of these are wrapped in a `prisma.$transaction()`. If the process crashes or the connection drops after step 1 but before step 3, all userServices pivot rows are gone but the service still exists, leaving the data in an inconsistent state. Step 2 (update managerId=null before deleting the row) is also logically redundant since the service is immediately deleted, but it still incurs an extra round-trip and is evidence of confused logic.
findings.root_cause: The delete operation was built incrementally without wrapping all writes in a single Prisma transaction, leaving the multi-step operation non-atomic.
findings.impact: A process crash or transient DB connection error mid-delete leaves `service` rows intact with orphaned (but deleted) `userService` rows, or a service row with managerId=null that was never cleaned up. Re-running the delete would then find zero userServices but a non-null managerId, producing an incoherent intermediate state that other reads could observe.
acceptance_criteria: DELETE /services/:id completes atomically — no partial state visible under concurrent reads
A simulated crash between the first and third writes leaves the service intact (rolled back)
findings.notes: The managerId=null update step (lines 332-337) is entirely redundant before delete — it should be removed along with the transaction wrapping fix.

findings.title: personal-todos.service.ts create() has TOCTOU race on the 20-todo limit
findings.description: The 20-todo limit is enforced by reading the count and then creating. There is no DB-level constraint capping the count per user, and no transaction around the count+create pair. Two concurrent requests for the same user can both read count=19 (or any value < 20), both pass the guard, and both successfully create a new todo, leaving the user with 21 todos. This violates the stated business invariant documented in CLAUDE.md ('Personal Todos: hard-coded 20-item limit').
findings.root_cause: The count-then-create pattern is inherently non-atomic in a concurrent environment without a transaction or a DB-level constraint to enforce the limit.
findings.impact: Any user who submits two or more simultaneous create requests (e.g., double-click, slow network retry, browser tab duplicate) can exceed the 20-item limit. The CLAUDE.md notes this as a hard-coded invariant, so the violation is a correctness regression.
acceptance_criteria: Concurrent double-submit for the same user does not result in more than 20 todos
The 21st todo attempt returns HTTP 400 even under concurrent load
findings.notes: CLAUDE.md explicitly states 'Personal Todos: hard-coded 20-item limit'. The findByUser() cleanup (cleanupOldCompleted) runs before the count check, which at least reduces the window, but does not close it.

findings.title: skills.service.ts findUsersBySkill() orderBy level:'desc' uses alphabetical string order, not semantic skill hierarchy
findings.description: The `findUsersBySkill()` method sorts results by `level: 'desc'` with the intent of showing the highest-skill users first. However, `level` is a Prisma enum stored as a PostgreSQL enum type; Prisma/Postgres sorts enum values alphabetically for `ORDER BY`. The SkillLevel enum values are `BEGINNER`, `INTERMEDIATE`, `EXPERT`, `MASTER`. Alphabetical descending order is: `MASTER (M) > INTERMEDIATE (I) > EXPERT (E) > BEGINNER (B)`. The correct semantic order (highest first) is `MASTER > EXPERT > INTERMEDIATE > BEGINNER`. The actual returned order places INTERMEDIATE before EXPERT, meaning intermediate-level users appear before expert-level users in the result set. The comment 'Experts en premier' is incorrect.
findings.root_cause: Relying on alphabetical ordering of string enum values instead of a semantically meaningful sort key.
findings.impact: Users querying 'who has this skill at EXPERT or above' get a list where INTERMEDIATE users are ranked above EXPERT users in the response, which is misleading for skill-matrix use cases.
acceptance_criteria: GET /skills/search/:skillId returns MASTER users before EXPERT users before INTERMEDIATE users before BEGINNER users
findings.notes: The levelOrder map at lines 461-466 already exists for the minLevel filter logic — it can be reused directly for sorting.

verified_clean: skills.service.ts: findAll() Promise.all([findMany, count]) — both queries use the same `where` clause, correct

skills.service.ts: assignSkillToUser() — proper upsert pattern (check existing, update or create), no TOCTOU risk because the unique key is (userId, skillId) and Prisma would throw P2002 only on a truly concurrent duplicate which would update correctly

skills.service.ts: validateImport() — sequential processing with seenNames Set avoids in-file duplicates, existingNames snapshot is taken before loop, no atomic write risk since this is read-only dry-run
skills.service.ts: importSkills() — each prisma.skill.create() is wrapped in try/catch at line 725, errors are recorded and execution continues
skills.service.ts: getSkillsMatrix() — Promise.all([users, skills]) is correct, both queries are independent reads

skills.service.ts: removeSkillFromUser() — findUnique then delete is fine, the delete will throw NotFoundException-equivalent if the row disappeared between the two calls (but it maps to NotFoundException on findUnique first)
departments.service.ts: create() — has isUniqueViolation catch handler wrapping prisma.department.create()
departments.service.ts: update() — has isUniqueViolation catch handler wrapping prisma.department.update()
departments.service.ts: remove() — single prisma.department.delete(), no multi-step; users/services count guard is a business invariant enforced before delete
departments.service.ts: getDepartmentStats() — read-only aggregate, no state changes
services.service.ts: create() — has isUniqueViolation catch wrapping prisma.service.create()
services.service.ts: update() — has isUniqueViolation catch wrapping prisma.service.update()
services.service.ts: findAll() Promise.all([findMany, count]) — both queries use same where clause, correct
services.service.ts: getServiceStats() — read-only aggregate
personal-todos.service.ts: update() — findUnique + ownership check + update is correct; no race since update is keyed on id (PK)
personal-todos.service.ts: delete() — findUnique + ownership check + delete is correct
personal-todos.service.ts: cleanupOldCompleted() — date math using setDate/getDate on UTC server is safe; completedAt is nullable and filtered correctly; no data corruption risk
personal-todos.service.ts: findByUser() — cleanup before read is non-atomic but acceptable (cleanup failure does not corrupt; at worst stale rows remain visible briefly)
All DTOs: class-validator decorators match business constraints (MaxLength, MinLength, IsEnum, IsUUID, IsOptional used correctly)
skills.controller.ts: route ordering — /me/assign and /me/remove/:skillId and /me/my-skills appear before /:id, correct NestJS route priority
departments.controller.ts: ParseUUIDPipe used on all :id params, no raw string injection risk
services.controller.ts: ParseUUIDPipe used on all :id params
personal-todos.controller.ts: ParseUUIDPipe used on :id param; CurrentUser injects authenticated user — no horizontal privilege escalation via URL manipulation
